summary: The post identifies a pattern where companies fund experimental or hard-to-monetize products through high-margin B2B contracts or ancillary businesses. Examples include B2C companies subsidized by B2B work, hardware companies funding R&D through software licensing, and Duolingo keeping its app free by selling crowd-sourced translations. The author draws a parallel to Amazon's strategy of offering unprofitable features to capture market share. She raises the question of whether this cross-subsidization model could fund socially valuable but hard-to-monetize products like ad-free social networks or journalism.
key_points: Several startups fund experimental consumer products through reliable, high-margin B2B contracts or adjacent business lines
Decoupling a product from its own profitability opens up the possibility of funding socially valuable but hard-to-monetize services
Duolingo exemplifies this model by selling crowd-sourced translations to keep its language-learning app free without resorting to ads
Public demand for ad-free social networks (App.net, Diaspora, Ello) demonstrates a market need that acquisitions address imperfectly
money_quotes: Basically, use a reliable and lucrative high-margin business to fund an experimental, lower-margin part of their business.
Decoupling products from their profitability offers other interesting implications.
What if a company used high-margin parts of their business to fund harder-to-ethically-monetize businesses like social networks, media companies, or journalism?
One wonders how they will produce an outsized return to investors without advertising.
Acquisitions can also lead to more conflicts of interest.
I'm curious whether more companies use this business model to fund products that are valuable to society, but are hard to monetize.
key_insight: Companies can use profitable business lines to cross-subsidize socially valuable products that are difficult to monetize directly, offering an alternative to ad-supported models.
